draft.Description: This concise topic summary addresses the practical challenges of measuring input latency on Linux, challenging common assumptions about X11 and Wayland performance. It highlights how VRR (Variable Refresh Rate) can introduce unexpected delays in high-refresh displays and how DXVK—while crucial for DirectX compatibility—may obscure true latency metrics. The summary avoids technical jargon, emphasizing real-world measurement pitfalls rather than theoretical comparisons. It argues that X11 often shows lower latency than Wayland in gaming scenarios due to simpler input path handling, contrary to popular claims that Wayland is inherently faster. The piece stresses that VRR implementations vary wildly across hardware and drivers, and DXVK’s translation overhead can inflate latency readings if not properly accounted for. This is critical for gamers and developers needing accurate input feedback. When does real-world input lag become a problem for your workflow?
draft.key_takeaways:
X11 typically has lower input latency than Wayland in gaming contexts
VRR adds variable latency depending on hardware and driver quality
DXVK translation can mask true latency metrics
Real-world measurement requires hardware-specific calibration
draft.technologies: X11, Wayland, VRR, DXVK, input latency measurement
problems_solved: Accurate input latency measurement on Linux
Understanding X11 vs Wayland latency differences
interesting_quotes: X11’s simpler input path often results in lower latency than Wayland in practice
VRR can add 10-30ms of latency depending on display and driver implementation
DXVK’s translation overhead may inflate latency readings if not properly calibrated
potential_discussion_topics: How should developers prioritize latency measurement tools when hardware varies?
When does real-world input lag become a problem for your workflow?
